text: Code refactoring is the process of improving a program's internal structure without changing how it works. The main goal is to make the code cleaner and more organized. For example, you might rename unclear variables, split a long function into smaller ones, or remove duplicated sections. Refactoring does not add new features or fix bugs—it only changes the code's layout. This practice helps reduce "technical debt," which is the extra work created by messy or rushed code. Regular refactoring also improves readability, making it easier for other developers (or your future self) to understand and modify the code. A well-structured codebase saves time and prevents errors in the long run.
